linkedInPosts.hook: Facilities managers don't ignore cold emails. They ignore lazy ones.
linkedInPosts.body:
I've reviewed hundreds of cold outreach messages sent by commercial cleaning companies.

The ones that get deleted in 3 seconds all look the same:

→ "We offer professional cleaning services at competitive rates."
→ "Our team is reliable and trustworthy."
→ "We'd love to discuss your cleaning needs."

These aren't pitches. They're noise.

Here's what a facilities manager is actually thinking when your email lands:

"Does this company understand what I'm responsible for?"
"Have they worked in buildings like mine?"
"Will they make my life easier or harder?"

Generic answers none of those questions.

A facilities manager overseeing a 40,000 sq ft office complex doesn't want to know you clean buildings.

They want to know you understand daily foot traffic, contractor sign-in procedures, after-hours access, and what happens when a cleaner doesn't show up.

That specificity is what gets a reply.
That specificity is what gets you on a shortlist.
That specificity is what wins the contract.

linkedInPosts.hook: 6 reasons your cold emails to facilities managers aren't converting (and what to fix):
linkedInPosts.body:
1. You opened with "I" instead of them
Every email starting with "I am writing to introduce..." loses before it starts. Open with a reference to their building, sector, or a problem they recognise.

2. You sent one email and gave up
Facilities managers manage dozens of supplier relationships. One email is not a sequence. A 3-touch sequence — spaced 4–6 days apart — dramatically increases reply rates.

3. You didn't mention their industry
A school bursar and an office facilities manager have completely different concerns. Compliance, infection control, term-time schedules, DBS requirements. If your email could apply to anyone, it applies to no one.

4. You listed services instead of outcomes
"We offer daily cleaning, washroom services, and waste removal" tells them nothing useful. "We help education facilities maintain cleaning compliance between inspections" tells them exactly what matters.

5. You asked for too much too soon
Asking for a meeting in the first email is like proposing on a first introduction. Ask for a 10-minute call or whether they have a contract review coming up.

6. You sent it from a Gmail address
A Gmail or Hotmail address signals you're a sole trader operating informally. Set up a branded domain email. It costs under £10/year and instantly increases credibility.

linkedInPosts.hook: She sent 47 cold emails last month. Got 1 reply — and it was "unsubscribe."
linkedInPosts.body:
Sarah runs a commercial cleaning company in the East Midlands.

She covers offices, schools, and light industrial sites. Her work is solid. Her retention rate is excellent. Her problem is the pipeline.

So she did what most cleaning company owners do when they decide to get proactive.

She built a list of local businesses. Found email addresses on Google. Wrote a template about her services. Sent it to 47 contacts over two weeks.

47 emails. 1 reply. 0 calls booked.

When I looked at her outreach, the issue was clear.

Every email was identical. Same subject line. Same body. Sent to a school headteacher, an office manager, and a warehouse operations lead — as if they all had the same pressures and priorities.

None of the emails mentioned the recipient's sector.
None referenced building size or likely cleaning frequency.
There was no follow-up sequence.
The call to action was "please get in touch if interested."

The facilities managers who received those emails didn't think Sarah was bad at her job.

They just didn't think she was relevant to them.

Two months later, after rebuilding her sequences with sector-specific messaging and a 3-email follow-up cadence, her reply rate was sitting at 11%.

Same market. Same services. Different message.

linkedInPosts.hook: Stop asking for referrals. Start building a contract pipeline you control.
linkedInPosts.body:
Referrals are great — when they come.

But building a commercial cleaning business on referrals is like building a house on sand.

You have no control over when they arrive.
You have no control over the quality of the lead.
You have no idea what next quarter looks like.

The cleaning companies I see scaling past £500k in recurring contract revenue aren't waiting for referrals.

They're running structured cold outreach to facilities managers in their target sectors.

Here's the uncomfortable truth most agencies won't tell you:

Cold email — done properly — outperforms referrals for new commercial contract acquisition.

Not because referrals are bad.
But because cold email is:

→ Predictable — you control the volume
→ Targeted — you choose the sectors, building types, and contract sizes
→ Scalable — more outreach equals more pipeline, month after month

A well-built 3-email sequence targeting healthcare facilities managers in your local area will consistently generate quote requests.

A referral might arrive in 6 months. Or it might not.

The cleaning companies that win the most contracts aren't the ones with the best reputation alone.

They're the ones who show up — in the inbox, at the right time, with the right message.

linkedInPosts.hook: How to write a cold email sequence that gets facilities managers to reply (3-email framework):
linkedInPosts.body:
Most cleaning companies send one email and call it outreach.

Here's a 3-email sequence that consistently generates replies from facilities managers in offices, healthcare, and education:

---

EMAIL 1 — The Relevant Introduction (Day 1)

Subject: Cleaning cover for [Building Type] in [Location]

Open by referencing something specific to their sector — not your business.

Example for healthcare: "Facilities managers in healthcare settings are increasingly being asked to evidence cleaning schedules for CQC compliance. We work with several NHS-adjacent sites in [region] to make that documentation straightforward."

Then: one sentence on what you do, one sentence on a relevant result, and a soft CTA — asking if they have a contract review scheduled in the next quarter.

---

EMAIL 2 — The Follow-Up With Value (Day 5)

Don't just say "following up on my last email."

Add something useful. A checklist. A question about their current cleaning frequency. A reference to a compliance requirement relevant to their sector.

Show you understand their world without a contract yet.

---

EMAIL 3 — The Honest Close (Day 10)

Keep it short. Acknowledge it may not be the right time. Give them an easy out — and an easy yes.

"If your current contract is working well, no action needed. If a review is coming up and you'd like an alternative quote, a 10-minute call is all it takes."

---

This sequence works because it's specific, it's patient, and it respects their time.

blogArticle.title: Cold Email for Commercial Cleaning: How to Win Facilities Managers With a 3-Email Sequence

seo.primaryKeyword: cold email for commercial cleaning companies
secondaryKeywords: how to get commercial cleaning contracts
seo.metaTitle: Cold Email for Commercial Cleaning Companies
seo.metaDescription: Stop sending generic pitches. Learn the 3-email sequence that wins facilities managers in offices, schools, and healthcare sites.
internalLinkSuggestions: How to Build a Commercial Cleaning Prospect List in Your Local Area
LinkedIn Outreach for Commercial Cleaning: How to Connect With Facilities Managers
How to Price Commercial Cleaning Contracts to Win and Retain Clients
localSeoVariations: cold email for commercial cleaning companies in Manchester
how to get commercial cleaning contracts in London

blogArticle.introduction:
Most cold emails sent by commercial cleaning companies get deleted in under three seconds. Not because facilities managers aren't looking for reliable contractors — they often are — but because the emails they receive all sound identical. "We offer professional cleaning services at competitive rates. Please find our brochure attached." That pitch lands in the bin every time.

Facilities managers responsible for offices, schools, and healthcare buildings are busy, sceptical, and drowning in generic supplier outreach. What cuts through isn't a louder pitch — it's a more specific one.

In this article, you'll learn why generic cold email fails in commercial cleaning, what facilities managers actually respond to, and how to build a 3-email sequence that references their industry, building type, and cleaning requirements. Done right, this approach converts at 3–5x the rate of a standard pitch — and builds a predictable pipeline of recurring commercial contracts.

sections.heading: Why Generic Cold Email Fails in Commercial Cleaning
sections.content:
Commercial cleaning is a crowded market. In most towns and cities, a facilities manager can choose from dozens of local contractors. When your email looks and reads the same as everyone else's, you're not competing — you're just adding noise.

The core problem is lack of specificity. A facilities manager at a GP surgery has entirely different concerns from an office manager at a 50-person accountancy firm. The surgery needs CQC-compliant cleaning protocols, colour-coded equipment, and documented audit trails. The office manager wants reliability, flexible scheduling around staff hours, and a single point of contact.

When you send both of them the same email, neither feels spoken to.

**Specificity signals competence.** When your email references a prospect's sector, mentions the likely size of their facility, and addresses the cleaning challenges specific to their building type, you immediately stand out from 90% of the competition. You're not just another cleaning company — you're a contractor who clearly understands their world.

This is the foundation of effective cold email for commercial cleaning: **research before outreach, always.**

sections.heading: The 3-Email Sequence That Wins Facilities Managers
sections.content:
A single cold email rarely wins a contract. A well-structured sequence — three emails sent over two to three weeks — creates familiarity, demonstrates persistence, and gives prospects multiple entry points to respond. Here's how to build it.

### Email 1: The Specific Hook (Day 1)

The first email has one job: earn a reply or a site visit request. Keep it short — under 150 words — and make it unmistakably specific to the recipient.

**Subject line examples:**
- *Healthcare cleaning for [Facility Name] — CQC-compliant contractors nearby*
- *Office cleaning in [Town] — reducing contractor changeover for [Company Name]*
- *School cleaning tender — [Borough] — are you reviewing suppliers this term?*

**Structure:**
- Opening line: Reference their specific building type or sector
- One sentence on a common pain point for that sector
- Two to three sentences on what you offer that directly addresses it
- A single, low-friction CTA: a site visit, a 10-minute call, or a tailored quote

**Example opening for a healthcare site:**
*"Most healthcare cleaning contractors in [City] struggle to maintain consistent infection control standards between staff shifts — particularly in high-touch areas like waiting rooms and treatment zones. We work exclusively with GP surgeries, dental practices, and outpatient facilities, and we can show you our CQC audit documentation on request."*

That's specific. That earns attention.

### Email 2: The Value Follow-Up (Day 7–9)

If there's no reply, send a follow-up that adds value rather than just nudging for a response. This is where most cleaning companies go wrong — they send a one-liner saying "just checking in." That wastes the opportunity.

Instead, include something useful:
- A brief checklist relevant to their sector (e.g., "5 things to check in your current cleaning contract before renewal")
- A reference to a recent compliance update relevant to their industry
- A short case study: one paragraph on a similar client, the problem they had, and what changed after switching to you

**Subject line examples:**
- *Quick checklist for reviewing your current cleaning contractor*
- *How we helped [similar business type] in [City] cut cleaning complaints by half*

Keep it under 200 words. End with the same low-friction CTA as email one.

### Email 3: The Soft Close (Day 14–18)

The third email is your last touch in this sequence. Keep it brief, direct, and non-pushy. Acknowledge that now might not be the right time — but leave the door open.

**Example:**
*"I'll keep this short. If your current cleaning setup is working well, no action needed. But if you're coming up to a contract review or have had reliability issues recently, we'd be happy to put together a no-obligation proposal. Happy to work around your schedule — just reply here and I'll take it from there."*

This email works because it respects their time, removes pressure, and plants a seed for when they are ready to switch.

**One rule across all three emails: never attach a brochure to a cold email.** It increases spam filter triggers and shifts the burden of effort onto the prospect. If they want more information, they'll ask — and that's the start of a conversation.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes Commercial Cleaning Companies Make With Cold Email
sections.content:
Even companies with a good service offering undermine their outreach with avoidable errors. Here are the most common ones.

**Sending from a free email address.** Gmail and Outlook addresses used for cold outreach destroy credibility instantly. Set up a professional domain email and warm it up properly before sending at volume.

**Writing about yourself, not the prospect.** "We have 10 years' experience and fully trained staff" tells the prospect nothing they care about. Reframe everything around their situation: what problem do they have, and how do you solve it?

**Targeting the wrong contact.** In smaller businesses, the office manager or practice manager makes cleaning decisions. In larger facilities, it's a dedicated facilities manager or FM contractor. In schools, it's often the school business manager or bursar. Sending to a generic "info@" address wastes your sequence entirely.

**Sending too many emails too quickly.** Back-to-back emails in the same week feel like spam. Space your sequence across two to three weeks to feel like a considered follow-up, not a mail blast.

**No personalisation at scale.** Using mail merge tools without checking the output creates embarrassing errors — wrong names, missing fields, broken formatting. Always QA a sample before sending any batch.

**Ignoring replies that aren't immediately positive.** A prospect who says "not right now" is a warm lead. Tag them, schedule a follow-up in 60–90 days, and touch base when their contract renewal window approaches.

sections.heading: Tools to Build and Run Your Cold Email Pipeline
sections.content:
You don't need expensive software to run a high-converting cold email operation. Here's a practical stack for commercial cleaning companies operating in local or regional markets.

**Prospect list building:**
- **Google Maps + LinkedIn** — Search for offices, schools, healthcare sites, and property management companies in your target area. Cross-reference with LinkedIn to find the right contact name and job title.
- **Apollo.io** — Useful for finding verified email addresses and filtering by industry, company size, and location. The free tier is sufficient for most local cleaning companies starting out.
- **Companies House** — For UK businesses, this reveals registered office addresses and director names, useful for targeting SMEs directly.

**Email sending and sequencing:**
- **Instantly.ai or Lemlist** — Both allow you to build automated sequences with personalisation variables, track open rates and replies, and manage follow-ups without manual effort.
- **Google Workspace (warmed domain)** — Use a sending domain separate from your main business domain to protect deliverability. Warm it up over two to three weeks before sending at volume.

**CRM and follow-up tracking:**
- **HubSpot Free CRM or Notion** — Track which prospects have been contacted, what stage they're at, and when to follow up. Even a simple spreadsheet beats no system at all.

**Before you invest in any tool, invest in your list quality.** A well-targeted list of 200 verified prospects in your local area will outperform a bought list of 2,000 unverified contacts every time. Precision beats volume in local commercial cleaning outreach.

blogArticle.conclusion:
Winning commercial cleaning contracts through cold email isn't about sending more — it's about sending better. Facilities managers respond to outreach that proves you understand their building, their sector, and their cleaning requirements before they've even spoken to you.

A 3-email sequence built around specificity, genuine follow-up value, and a respectful close will consistently outperform generic pitches. Pair it with a targeted local prospect list, a professional sending setup, and a simple CRM to track replies, and you have the foundation of a predictable contract pipeline.

The cleaning companies winning the best recurring contracts in 2024 aren't the biggest — they're the most deliberate about how they approach new business. Start there.

---

**Ready to build a cold email system that wins commercial contracts in your area?**

blogArticle.fullMarkdown:
# Cold Email for Commercial Cleaning: How to Win Facilities Managers With a 3-Email Sequence

Most cold emails sent by commercial cleaning companies get deleted in under three seconds. Not because facilities managers aren't looking for reliable contractors — they often are — but because the emails they receive all sound identical. "We offer professional cleaning services at competitive rates. Please find our brochure attached." That pitch lands in the bin every time.

Facilities managers responsible for offices, schools, and healthcare buildings are busy, sceptical, and drowning in generic supplier outreach. What cuts through isn't a louder pitch — it's a more specific one.

In this article, you'll learn why generic cold email fails in commercial cleaning, what facilities managers actually respond to, and how to build a 3-email sequence that references their industry, building type, and cleaning requirements. Done right, this approach converts at 3–5x the rate of a standard pitch — and builds a predictable pipeline of recurring commercial contracts.

---

## Why Generic Cold Email Fails in Commercial Cleaning

Commercial cleaning is a crowded market. In most towns and cities, a facilities manager can choose from dozens of local contractors. When your email looks and reads the same as everyone else's, you're not competing — you're just adding noise.

The core problem is lack of specificity. A facilities manager at a GP surgery has entirely different concerns from an office manager at a 50-person accountancy firm. The surgery needs CQC-compliant cleaning protocols, colour-coded equipment, and documented audit trails. The office manager wants reliability, flexible scheduling around staff hours, and a single point of contact.

When you send both of them the same email, neither feels spoken to.

**Specificity signals competence.** When your email references a prospect's sector, mentions the likely size of their facility, and addresses the cleaning challenges specific to their building type, you immediately stand out from 90% of the competition. You're not just another cleaning company — you're a contractor who clearly understands their world.

This is the foundation of effective cold email for commercial cleaning: **research before outreach, always.**

---

## The 3-Email Sequence That Wins Facilities Managers

A single cold email rarely wins a contract. A well-structured sequence — three emails sent over two to three weeks — creates familiarity, demonstrates persistence, and gives prospects multiple entry points to respond. Here's how to build it.

### Email 1: The Specific Hook (Day 1)

The first email has one job: earn a reply or a site visit request. Keep it short — under 150 words — and make it unmistakably specific to the recipient.

**Subject line examples:**
- *Healthcare cleaning for [Facility Name] — CQC-compliant contractors nearby*
- *Office cleaning in [Town] — reducing contractor changeover for [Company Name]*
- *School cleaning tender — [Borough] — are you reviewing suppliers this term?*

**Structure:**
- Opening line: Reference their specific building type or sector
- One sentence on a common pain point for that sector
- Two to three sentences on what you offer that directly addresses it
- A single, low-friction CTA: a site visit, a 10-minute call, or a tailored quote

**Example opening for a healthcare site:**

*"Most healthcare cleaning contractors in [City] struggle to maintain consistent infection control standards between staff shifts — particularly in high-touch areas like waiting rooms and treatment zones. We work exclusively with GP surgeries, dental practices, and outpatient facilities, and we can show you our CQC audit documentation on request."*

That's specific. That earns attention.

### Email 2: The Value Follow-Up (Day 7–9)

If there's no reply, send a follow-up that adds value rather than just nudging for a response. This is where most cleaning companies go wrong — they send a one-liner saying "just checking in." That wastes the opportunity.

Instead, include something useful:
- A brief checklist relevant to their sector (e.g., "5 things to check in your current cleaning contract before renewal")
- A reference to a recent compliance update relevant to their industry
- A short case study: one paragraph on a similar client, the problem they had, and what changed after switching to you

**Subject line examples:**
- *Quick checklist for reviewing your current cleaning contractor*
- *How we helped [similar business type] in [City] cut cleaning complaints by half*

Keep it under 200 words. End with the same low-friction CTA as email one.

### Email 3: The Soft Close (Day 14–18)

The third email is your last touch in this sequence. Keep it brief, direct, and non-pushy. Acknowledge that now might not be the right time — but leave the door open.

**Example:**

*"I'll keep this short. If your current cleaning setup is working well, no action needed. But if you're coming up to a contract review or have had reliability issues recently, we'd be happy to put together a no-obligation proposal. Happy to work around your schedule — just reply here and I'll take it from there."*

This email works because it respects their time, removes pressure, and plants a seed for when they are ready to switch.

**One rule across all three emails: never attach a brochure to a cold email.** It increases spam filter triggers and shifts the burden of effort onto the prospect. If they want more information, they'll ask — and that's the start of a conversation.

---

## Common Mistakes Commercial Cleaning Companies Make With Cold Email

Even companies with a good service offering undermine their outreach with avoidable errors. Here are the most common ones.

**Sending from a free email address.** Gmail and Outlook addresses used for cold outreach destroy credibility instantly. Set up a professional domain email and warm it up properly before sending at volume.

**Writing about yourself, not the prospect.** "We have 10 years' experience and fully trained staff" tells the prospect nothing they care about. Reframe everything around their situation: what problem do they have, and how do you solve it?

**Targeting the wrong contact.** In smaller businesses, the office manager or practice manager makes cleaning decisions. In larger facilities, it's a dedicated facilities manager or FM contractor. In schools, it's often the school business manager or bursar. Sending to a generic "info@" address wastes your sequence entirely.

**Sending too many emails too quickly.** Back-to-back emails in the same week feel like spam. Space your sequence across two to three weeks to feel like a considered follow-up, not a mail blast.

**No personalisation at scale.** Using mail merge tools without checking the output creates embarrassing errors — wrong names, missing fields, broken formatting. Always QA a sample before sending any batch.

**Ignoring replies that aren't immediately positive.** A prospect who says "not right now" is a warm lead. Tag them, schedule a follow-up in 60–90 days, and touch base when their contract renewal window approaches.

---

## Tools to Build and Run Your Cold Email Pipeline

You don't need expensive software to run a high-converting cold email operation. Here's a practical stack for commercial cleaning companies operating in local or regional markets.

### Prospect List Building

- **Google Maps + LinkedIn** — Search for offices, schools, healthcare sites, and property management companies in your target area. Cross-reference with LinkedIn to find the right contact name and job title.
- **Apollo.io** — Useful for finding verified email addresses and filtering by industry, company size, and location. The free tier is sufficient for most local cleaning companies starting out.
- **Companies House** — For UK businesses, this reveals registered office addresses and director names, useful for targeting SMEs directly.

### Email Sending and Sequencing

- **Instantly.ai or Lemlist** — Both allow you to build automated sequences with personalisation variables, track open rates and replies, and manage follow-ups without manual effort.
- **Google Workspace (warmed domain)** — Use a sending domain separate from your main business domain to protect deliverability. Warm it up over two to three weeks before sending at volume.

### CRM and Follow-Up Tracking

- **HubSpot Free CRM or Notion** — Track which prospects have been contacted, what stage they're at, and when to follow up. Even a simple spreadsheet beats no system at all.

**Before you invest in any tool, invest in your list quality.** A well-targeted list of 200 verified prospects in your local area will outperform a bought list of 2,000 unverified contacts every time. Precision beats volume in local commercial cleaning outreach.

---

## Start Building a Pipeline, Not Just Sending Emails

Winning commercial cleaning contracts through cold email isn't about sending more — it's about sending better. Facilities managers respond to outreach that proves you understand their building, their sector, and their cleaning requirements before they've even spoken to you.

A 3-email sequence built around specificity, genuine follow-up value, and a respectful close will consistently outperform generic pitches. Pair it with a targeted local prospect list, a professional sending setup, and a simple CRM to track replies, and you have the foundation of a predictable contract pipeline.

The cleaning companies winning the best recurring contracts aren't the biggest — they're the most deliberate about how they approach new business. Start there.

---

### Ready to Build a Cold Email System That Wins Commercial Contracts in Your Area?

Book a free cold email strategy call with CleanReach. We'll review your current outreach, identify the highest-value prospect segments in your local market, and give you a sequence framework you can deploy immediately.

**[Book your free strategy call →](https://api.leadconnectorhq.com/widget/bookings/call-with-cleanreach)**

emailNewsletter.subjectLine: Why your cold emails get ignored
emailNewsletter.previewText: Facilities managers delete generic pitches. Here's what they respond to instead.
emailNewsletter.body:
Most cold emails cleaning companies send get deleted in under three seconds.

Not because the service is bad. Because the email reads like it was written for anyone — which means it feels relevant to no one.

Facilities managers in offices, schools, and healthcare buildings receive outreach constantly. They're not looking for the cheapest quote or the longest list of services. They're looking for a contractor who clearly understands their environment.

That's the gap most cleaning companies miss.

When your email references their specific building type, the cleaning challenges that come with it, and the frequency that makes sense for their operation — it signals competence before you've even had a conversation. It shows you've done the work.

A 3-email sequence built around this principle — industry, building size, cleaning frequency — consistently converts at 3 to 5 times the rate of a generic pitch. The first email opens the door. The second adds credibility. The third asks for a specific next step.

This isn't complicated. But it does require you to stop sending the same email to every prospect and start treating each vertical as its own conversation.

A healthcare facility manager worries about infection control and compliance sign-off. An office building manager wants consistency and minimal disruption. A school facilities lead needs to work around term times and safeguarding requirements. These are different people with different problems — and your outreach should reflect that.

If your pipeline is quiet right now, the fix probably isn't more volume. It's more relevance.

Take 30 minutes to map out what each of your target verticals actually cares about. Then write three short emails — under 150 words each — that speak directly to those concerns. Send them on a 5-day cadence. Track replies, not open rates.

That's a cold email sequence worth running.

gbpPosts.body:
Most facilities managers don't realise how much time they waste chasing unreliable cleaning contractors.

Late arrivals. Missed areas. No account manager to call. It creates extra work for your team — and complaints you shouldn't have to deal with.

Our contract cleaning services are built specifically for commercial environments: offices, healthcare facilities, schools, and managed properties in [CITY].

What you get:
— A dedicated point of contact
— Scheduled cleans tailored to your building size and occupancy
— Consistent standards verified by regular site checks
— Full insurance and compliance documentation on request

We don't offer one-size-fits-all. Every quote is scoped to your facility, your hours, and your cleaning frequency requirements.

If you manage a building in [CITY] and you're tired of contractors who treat commercial sites like residential jobs, we should talk.

Get a scoped commercial cleaning quote for your facility today.
gbpPosts.cta: Book a free cold email strategy call at api.leadconnectorhq.com/widget/bookings/call-with-cleanreach

gbpPosts.body:
A facilities manager at a 4-floor office complex in [CITY] came to us after their previous janitorial contractor failed a health and safety audit.

The problems: inconsistent clean logs, no COSHH documentation, and staff rotating weekly with no site familiarity.

Within two weeks of switching to our janitorial services, they had:
— A named cleaning operative assigned to their site
— Digital clean logs accessible on request
— Full COSHH folders and risk assessments in place
— Zero complaints from building tenants in the following quarter

Audit passed. Facilities manager stopped fielding complaints. Building tenants noticed the difference.

This is what professional commercial cleaning looks like when it's matched to the actual demands of a managed facility.

If your current office cleaning contractors are creating more admin than they're removing, it's worth getting a second opinion.

We offer a no-obligation site assessment and quote for commercial buildings across [CITY].

gbpPosts.body:
Facilities managers in [CITY] shortlist cleaning contractors differently to how most companies pitch.

They're not looking for the cheapest quote. They're looking for a contractor who understands their building type, their compliance requirements, and their cleaning schedule — before the first call.

Generic outreach gets ignored. So does a contractor who shows up without knowing the difference between cleaning a GP surgery and cleaning a school corridor.

As a commercial cleaning company working across offices, healthcare, and education sites in [CITY], we scope every enquiry by:
— Building type and square footage
— Foot traffic and infection control requirements
— Cleaning frequency and out-of-hours access
— Compliance and auditing expectations

This specificity is why facilities managers who contact us tend to move forward — they're not being sold to, they're being understood.

If you're a facilities or office manager in [CITY] currently reviewing your facilities cleaning services contract, we'll give you a straight assessment of what your building actually needs.

videoScripts.hook: Facilities managers are ignoring your cold emails — here's exactly why.
videoScripts.problem: You're sending cold emails to facilities managers and getting nothing back. No replies, no calls, no contracts. And it's not because they don't need a cleaning company — it's because your email looks exactly like the last ten they deleted.
videoScripts.solution: Facilities managers shortlist contractors based on two things: responsiveness and specificity. If your email doesn't reference their industry, their building type, or their likely cleaning schedule — it reads as spam. What actually works is a 3-email sequence. Email one references their sector — office, healthcare, or education — and a specific pain point for that environment. Email two follows up with a concrete offer tied to their building size. Email three is a short, direct close. Each email does one job. No long pitches. No brochure attachments.
videoScripts.proof: This kind of targeted sequence converts at three to five times the rate of a generic pitch — because it signals that you actually understand their operation before you've even walked through the door.
videoScripts.cta: If you want us to build this sequence for your cleaning business, book a free cold email strategy call. Link is in the comments.
videoScripts.fullScript:
[0–3 seconds] Facilities managers are ignoring your cold emails — here's exactly why.

[3–15 seconds] You're sending cold emails to facilities managers and getting nothing back. No replies, no calls, no contracts. And it's not because they don't need a cleaning company — it's because your email looks exactly like the last ten they deleted.

[15–55 seconds] Facilities managers shortlist contractors based on two things: responsiveness and specificity. If your email doesn't reference their industry, their building type, or their likely cleaning schedule — it reads as spam. What actually works is a 3-email sequence. Email one references their sector — office, healthcare, or education — and a specific pain point for that environment. Email two follows up with a concrete offer tied to their building size. Email three is a short, direct close. Each email does one job. No long pitches. No brochure attachments.

[55–70 seconds] This kind of targeted sequence converts at three to five times the rate of a generic pitch — because it signals that you actually understand their operation before you've even walked through the door.

[70–82 seconds] If you want us to build this sequence for your cleaning business, book a free cold email strategy call. Link is in the comments.

videoScripts.hook: Your cold emails aren't failing because of competition — they're failing because they're generic.
videoScripts.problem: Facilities managers get pitched by cleaning companies every week. If your email could've been sent to anyone, it gets ignored by everyone.
videoScripts.solution: Here's what actually gets replies. Send a 3-part email sequence. First email — name their industry. Healthcare? Mention infection control. Schools? Mention high-traffic restrooms. Second email — reference their building size and what a realistic cleaning schedule looks like. Third email — short, direct, one question close. That's it. Specific industry. Specific building. Specific ask.
videoScripts.proof: Targeted sequences like this convert three to five times better than a generic pitch. Because specificity signals you know what you're doing before they've even met you.
videoScripts.cta: Want the full sequence built for your business? Book a free strategy call — link in bio.

coldEmailAngles.valueProposition: By right-sizing cleaning frequency to actual building usage — not a generic schedule — we deliver better coverage at a lower annual cost, with full audit trails for compliance.
coldEmailAngles.cta: Happy to show you the breakdown — are you the right person at [Company] to have that conversation?
